title: Rocket stove principles: efficient wood combustion in a small mass
subtitle: The L-shaped combustion chamber, insulated burn tunnel, and why it outperforms an open fire
excerpt: A rocket stove burns small-diameter wood in an L-shaped combustion chamber with an insulated horizontal feed tube and a short vertical riser. The design creates a strong draft that produces near-complete combustion at high temperature. Fuel consumption is 70-80% lower than an open fire for the same heat output.
sourceNotes: Ianto Evans and Leslie Jackson 'Rocket Mass Heaters' (3rd ed., 2014). Aprovecho Research Center publications on biomass combustion efficiency. TLUD (Top-Lit Updraft) and rocket stove comparative efficiency data from Aprovecho.

glossaryTerms.definition: A wood-burning cooking or heating device based on an L-shaped combustion chamber with a short insulated horizontal feed tube and a taller vertical riser. The geometry and insulation produce a strong natural draft and high combustion temperature, resulting in efficient burning and low smoke output compared with open fires or simple pit cooking.

The area at the top of the horizontal feed tube and the base of the riser where the fuel meets pre-heated secondary air. In a rocket stove, the combustion zone temperature reaches 800-1000°C, burning off volatile gases that produce smoke in lower-temperature fires.

content.text: 's efficiency comes from three design principles working together: the L-shape creates a strong natural draft; insulation at the
maintains high temperature without constant stoking; and the top-feeding fuel design means only the tip of each piece of wood burns at any time, reducing the surface area in contact with the fire.

For a small cooking rocket stove (suitable for a single pot): the feed tube cross-section should be square or circular with an internal area of approximately 70-100 cm2 (corresponding to about a 9-10 cm diameter). The riser height should be 2-4 times the feed tube length. The feed tube and riser junction must be insulated: firebricks, perlite-clay mix, or cob (clay and sand) maintain the combustion zone temperature. Metal alone conducts heat away and reduces efficiency.

A rocket stove burns small-diameter wood: branches 25-75 mm diameter, split if larger. The small diameter ensures rapid ignition and complete combustion. Dry wood is essential: moisture content above 20% produces steam that cools the combustion zone and increases smoke. Wood should be stored in a dry, ventilated space for at least 12 months after cutting.

A well-built 10 cm rocket stove generates approximately 5-8 kW of heat output, sufficient to boil 5 litres of water in 10-15 minutes. The pot sits directly on the riser top. Because combustion is nearly complete, visible smoke is minimal after the first 2-3 minutes of startup. Cooking in a still location with no wind protection is straightforward; in wind, a windbreak on three sides significantly improves performance by maintaining the draft.

A rocket stove produces combustion gases including carbon monoxide. Do not use inside a building, tent, or enclosed space. Use outdoors with adequate ventilation. Keep a safe clearance from combustible materials around and above the riser.
